w:r.w:t: This document is the build-ready database specification for V2. Every table, column, data type, relationship, and access control rule is defined here. On the first day of the summer build, Claude Code receives this document and builds the Supabase database to match it exactly — no schema design decisions are made during the build.

Complete table definitions — every column, data type, and constraint. Relationship map. Architecture decisions log. Row Level Security rules. dbdiagram.io schema definition. Claude Code handoff prompt.

Five decisions were made before schema design began. Each decision is recorded below with the option chosen and the rationale. These decisions are locked — they should not be revisited during the build without a specific, documented reason.

Members access only current phase and below. Sequential logic is core to clinical credibility — members who can read ahead treat phases as optional rather than prerequisite. A programme overview page describes each phase at a high level without unlocking content.

One column per question response plus stored computed scores. Test is stable and rarely changes. Individual columns make research queries trivial SQL. Computed scores (tmj_score, cervical_score, classification) stored at test completion so the dashboard never recalculates them.

progress_log_triggers table with log_id and trigger_id. Research queries across triggers are trivial joins. New triggers added to the reference table without schema changes. Array storage would require UNNEST() for every aggregation query.

Single boolean on memberships table. Access check: if is_founding_member = TRUE, grant access immediately and never query Stripe. Unambiguous, no string comparisons, no separate tables. Stripe columns remain NULL for founding members permanently.

PII lives only on the users table. All health data tables (progress_logs, phase1_assessment) link by user_id only and contain no PII. On deletion request: strip PII from users table, health data survives with anonymous user_id. Research dataset grows monotonically and never shrinks. RLS stays simple throughout active membership.

Core identity table. Stores authentication identifiers, intake test data (Layer 1), and computed classification. Supabase Auth manages password hashing and sessions — this table extends the auth.users record. PII is limited to this table by design: name and email live here only, enabling clean anonymisation on deletion.
Supabase Auth user ID. Foreign key to auth.users.
PII. Only email storage location in the schema.
First name or chosen display name. Used in dashboard greeting.
Optional short bio shown on community profile page.
Last profile update. Updated via trigger on row change.
Neck curl on floor response. 0/2/4. Highest weighted movement question.
TRUE if member selected asymmetric response — one side noticeably worse than other. Separate from m4_score.
Morning jaw soreness or tinnitus correlates. 0/1/2.
Headaches — both yes and sometimes score 1. 0/1.
Pre-test noise exposure question. Unscored. Modifies result page text if TRUE.
Sum of M1-M5. Max 16. Stored at test completion.
Sum of S1-S8. Max 17. Stored at test completion.
A, B, or C. Stored at test completion.
TRUE if any single movement question scored its maximum value.
Which question triggered flag_single_max_movement. e.g. M3.
Duplicate of m4_asymmetric for fast flag lookup on result page.
Timestamp when test was submitted. NULL until test completed.

Stores all in-platform Phase 1 deep assessment data. One row per member, created when they begin Phase 1 and updated as they progress through modules. This is health data under UK GDPR and receives its own RLS policy. Intake test responses (M1-M5, S1-S8) are imported automatically from the users table — they are not re-entered here but contribute to TMJ and cervical module scores.
Links to users table. No PII stored in this table.
Jaw drift present on slow controlled opening.
left / right. Recorded when tmj_jaw_drift = TRUE.
left / right. Recorded when tmj_masseter_asymmetry = TRUE.
Any joint sounds on opening/closing (clicking, popping, grating).
Maximum opening restriction noted on finger-stacking assessment.
Morning jaw soreness or facial tension on waking.
Jaw pain or aching when eating or yawning.
Tinnitus noticeably worse after prolonged chewing.
History of orthodontic treatment, braces, or retainers.
History of dental extractions, particularly wisdom teeth.
History of jaw surgery or significant dental procedures.
History of jaw injury or direct trauma.
Sum of all TMJ module indicator scores including imported M1/M2.
tmj_raw_score as percentage of module maximum. Used for profile type classification.
Tenderness asymmetric between sides — more significant finding.
SCM tone or tenderness asymmetry between sides.
Rotation range restriction or asymmetry between sides.
Forward head posture confirmed via side profile self-assessment.
Clear tinnitus reduction after 10 minutes supine. Positive = significant cervical driver indicator.
Regular neck pain, stiffness, or restricted movement.
Headaches at base of skull or spreading from neck.
Tinnitus noticeably worse after prolonged desk work or driving.
History of whiplash or significant neck injury.
History of prolonged sedentary occupation or sustained poor desk posture.
History of one-sided sport or activity asymmetrically loading the neck.
Sum of all cervical module indicator scores including imported M3/M4/M5.
cerv_raw_score as percentage of module maximum.
left / right. Correlated with masseter asymmetry findings.
Significant sustained desk posture load confirmed.
Asymmetric exercise or movement patterns confirmed.
Tinnitus clearly tracking stress — noticeable increase during anxious periods.
Habitual monitoring of tinnitus loudness, difficulty not attending to it.
Difficulty falling asleep due to tinnitus or noticeably louder on waking.
Awareness of tinnitus triggering anxiety which amplifies it.
TRUE if jaw drifts away from the ear with louder tinnitus. Flagged with scientific uncertainty note.
TRUE if TMJ score exceeds minimum assignment threshold.
TRUE if cervical score exceeds minimum assignment threshold.
Generated personalised paragraph. Contextual flags modify output text.

Daily check-in log. One row per submission. Health data under UK GDPR — RLS enforced. Contains no PII. user_id is the only link to identity. On deletion request, user_id is anonymised (set to a fixed anonymous sentinel value or NULL depending on anonymisation implementation) but rows are retained for the research dataset.
No PII in this table. Only link to identity.
The date this log covers. One log per user per date enforced by UNIQUE(user_id, log_date).
Primary outcome variable. The central research metric.
Second confounding variable. Low sleep reliably worsens tinnitus.
Free text optional field. Qualitative signal at scale reveals patterns no tag list catches.
UNIQUE constraint on (user_id, log_date) prevents duplicate logs for the same day.
Trigger data is stored in progress_log_triggers junction table — see Section 2.5.
Reference table for predefined trigger options. Seeded at build time. Adding new trigger options is a single INSERT here — no schema changes required.
Small integer ID for efficient junction table storage.
Display name. e.g. stress, jaw clenching, poor sleep, alcohol, long desk session, dental work, illness, travel, exercise, other.

Junction table linking progress logs to selected triggers. Many-to-many relationship. Research query pattern: join to progress_logs on log_id, join to triggers on trigger_id, filter and aggregate cleanly.
NOT NULL, FK progress_logs(id) ON DELETE CASCADE
Deleting a log removes all its trigger associations.
Reference to the trigger type selected.
PRIMARY KEY is composite: (log_id, trigger_id). Prevents duplicate trigger selections on the same log.
Tracks each member's position in the five-phase framework. One row per member. Created on first framework access. Sequential unlocking: access check is requested_phase <= current_phase.
One row per member. UNIQUE enforces this.
Active phase. 1-5. Access check: requested_phase <= current_phase.
Active session within current_phase. Resets to 1 on phase advance.
Session structure choice for Phase 3. 1=Sequential, 2=Parallel, 3=Prioritised Parallel. NULL until member reaches Phase 3.
Copied from phase1_assessment on Phase 1 completion. Used by framework content system to determine which Phase 3 content to serve.
Copied from phase1_assessment on Phase 1 completion.
Individual phase completion timestamps. NULL until completed.
When the member first accessed the framework.
Last progress update. Updated via trigger.

Membership and payment state. One row per member. The is_founding_member boolean is the primary access gate — if TRUE, Stripe columns are never read and access is granted immediately. Founding members have NULL Stripe columns permanently.
Primary access gate. TRUE = grant access immediately, never query Stripe. Set to TRUE at account creation for founding members.
Stripe customer ID. NULL for founding members.
Active subscription ID. NULL for founding members.
active / cancelled / past_due / trialing. For founding members, always active.
founding / paid. Useful for conversion rate queries alongside is_founding_member.
Set when a paid member cancels. NULL for active and founding members.
Conversion query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM memberships WHERE is_founding_member = TRUE AND stripe_subscription_id IS NOT NULL — founding members who chose to pay.
Every foreign key relationship in the schema. All health data tables link to users by user_id only — no PII stored in health tables.
One-to-one. One Phase 1 assessment per member.
One-to-many. Many daily logs per member.
Many-to-one. Many trigger selections per log. CASCADE DELETE.
One-to-one. One progress record per member.
One-to-one. One membership record per member.

RLS must be enabled on every table listed below before any member data is stored. These rules enforce at the database level that members can only read and write their own data. They are the primary security control for the platform. Test explicitly with a second account before launch: verify that account B cannot read account A's progress_logs, phase1_assessment, or framework_progress in any way.
SELECT: auth.uid() = id — members can read only their own row.
UPDATE: auth.uid() = id — members can update only their own row. Email update handled separately via Supabase Auth.
INSERT: handled by Supabase Auth trigger on signup. Members never INSERT directly.
DELETE: restricted to service role only. Deletion is handled by the anonymisation handler, not by the member directly.
SELECT: auth.uid() = user_id — members read only their own assessment.
INSERT: auth.uid() = user_id — members create their own assessment row.
UPDATE: auth.uid() = user_id — members update their own assessment as they progress through Phase 1 modules.
DELETE: restricted to service role only.
SELECT: auth.uid() = user_id — members read only their own logs.
INSERT: auth.uid() = user_id — members create their own log entries.
UPDATE: auth.uid() = user_id — members can edit their own logs. Consider restricting to same-day edits only.
DELETE: restricted to service role only.
SELECT: policy via progress_logs — user must own the parent log. Implement as: EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM progress_logs WHERE progress_logs.id = log_id AND progress_logs.user_id = auth.uid()).
INSERT: same EXISTS check as SELECT.
DELETE: same EXISTS check. Also handled by CASCADE from progress_logs.
SELECT: public read — all authenticated members can read the triggers reference list. No sensitive data here.
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE: restricted to service role only. Trigger options are managed by admin, not members.
INSERT: auth.uid() = user_id — row created on first framework access.
DELETE: restricted to service role only.
SELECT: auth.uid() = user_id — members can read their own membership status.
INSERT: restricted to service role only. Memberships are created by the server after Stripe confirmation, never directly by the member.
UPDATE: restricted to service role only. Status updates come from Stripe webhooks via server-side handler, never from the client.
DELETE: restricted to service role only.

The memberships table INSERT and UPDATE policies are the most security-sensitive rules in the schema. If a member could INSERT their own membership row or UPDATE their own status, they could grant themselves free access or reactivate a cancelled subscription. These operations must flow exclusively through server-side handlers triggered by Stripe webhooks. Verify this explicitly: attempt to INSERT a memberships row from the client with a valid session and confirm it is rejected.
Copy the block below in its entirety into dbdiagram.io to generate a visual entity-relationship diagram. Use this diagram as a reference throughout the build.
user_id uuid [unique, not null, ref: - users.id]
user_id uuid [unique, not null, ref: - users.id]

Copy this prompt exactly to open the V2 database build session in Claude Code. Paste it in full — do not summarise or paraphrase. The schema table definitions from Section 2 should be appended where indicated.
You are helping me build the V2 platform for the Somatic Tinnitus Project. Today's task is the complete Supabase database setup.

TASK: Build the Supabase database exactly matching the schema below. Do not improvise column names, types, or relationships. Do not add tables or columns not listed here. Work table by table in the order given.
BEFORE WRITING ANY CODE, explain what you are about to build and your approach. After each table is created, confirm it matches the spec before moving to the next.
- Auth: Supabase Auth — users table extends auth.users
1. Enable Row Level Security on ALL tables except triggers (which is public read).
2. RLS policies are specified per table below. Implement them exactly as written.
3. The memberships table INSERT and UPDATE must be service role only — members must never be able to modify their own membership status from the client.
4. After all tables and policies are created, we will test with a second account that account B cannot read account A's progress_logs, phase1_assessment, or framework_progress.
1. users — extends auth.users, stores intake test data and computed classification
2. phase1_assessment — in-platform Phase 1 deep assessment, one row per member
3. progress_logs — daily tracker submissions, one row per member per date
4. triggers — reference table for trigger tag options, seeded at build time
5. progress_log_triggers — junction table, links logs to selected triggers
6. framework_progress — phase and session position tracking, one row per member
7. memberships — membership state and Stripe integration, one row per member
[PASTE FULL TABLE DEFINITIONS FROM DOCUMENT 7 SECTION 2 HERE]
users: SELECT/UPDATE where auth.uid() = id. INSERT via Auth trigger. DELETE service role only.
phase1_assessment: SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE where auth.uid() = user_id. DELETE service role only.
progress_logs: SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE where auth.uid() = user_id. DELETE service role only.
progress_log_triggers: SELECT/INSERT/DELETE where EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM progress_logs WHERE id = log_id AND user_id = auth.uid()).
triggers: SELECT public (all authenticated). INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE service role only.
framework_progress: SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE where auth.uid() = user_id. DELETE service role only.
memberships: SELECT where auth.uid() = user_id. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE service role only.
After tables are created, seed the triggers reference table with these values:
stress, poor sleep, alcohol, jaw clenching, long desk session, dental work, illness, travel, exercise, other
1. updated_at trigger on users — sets updated_at = NOW() on any row update.
3. UNIQUE constraint on progress_logs(user_id, log_date) — prevents duplicate daily logs.

Show me the complete SQL that was run so I can review it. Flag the three most important lines to check in the RLS policies. We will then test with a second account before moving to authentication setup.
1. Open a new Claude Code session at the start of the summer build.
2. Paste the prompt above in full, then append the full table definitions from Section 2 of this document where indicated.
3. Before Claude Code writes a single line of SQL, ask it to explain its approach. Read the explanation before saying proceed.
4. After the build, review the RLS policies specifically. These are the lines most likely to contain subtle logical errors.
5. Test with a second account before proceeding to authentication. This is non-negotiable.
Built to help people. Designed to last.
THE SOMATIC TINNITUS PROJECT — V2 Database Schema & Architecture Decisions — Document 7
